summary: Gruber dismantles a BusinessWeek article that portrays Universal Music chief Doug Morris's 'Total Music' subscription service as a clever challenge to iTunes. The scheme would have hardware makers or carriers pay a $5/month subscription fee per device, supposedly making music 'essentially free' for consumers. Gruber argues the cost would obviously be passed to consumers as a ~$90 surcharge on devices, making it just a pre-paid subscription service dressed up as innovation. He also catches BusinessWeek falsely implying Universal can't sell DRM-free music through iTunes, when Universal itself chose not to use iTunes Plus. The piece highlights the irony that Total Music would almost certainly require its own DRM while the industry complains about Apple's.
key_points: The $5/month subscription fee would translate to ~$90 added to device prices, not magically 'absorbed' by manufacturers
BusinessWeek falsely implies Universal can't sell DRM-free music on iTunes, when Universal voluntarily chose not to use iTunes Plus
Total Music would almost certainly require its own DRM, making the industry's complaints about Apple's DRM hypocritical
The claimed 'precedent' of Microsoft paying $1 per Zune sold bears no resemblance to a 90-fold increase to $90 per device
The article is essentially hailing Doug Morris as a genius for inventing a pre-paid subscription service
money_quotes: So hardware makers will 'absorb the cost', rather than passing the cost along to consumers. Uh-huh.
Good rule of thumb: the more an article attributes an Apple product or service to Steve Jobs personally, the more likely it's a hatchet job.
That freedom is available to buyers of iTunes songs, in the form of Apple's DRM-free iTunes Plus, which Universal Music has chosen not to sell.
The hypocrisy here is running thick.
Thank you for performing the basic arithmetic for me.
So, apparently, this $90 is going to appear out of thin air.
So, a 90-fold increase has 'precedent' in a one dollar per unit fee. Uh-huh.
So what this boils down to is BusinessWeek hailing Doug Morris as genius for coming up with the idea of a pre-paid subscription service.
key_insight: Universal Music's 'Total Music' scheme is just a pre-paid subscription service repackaged as innovation, with costs inevitably passed to consumers despite being marketed as free.
